Tool Call: retrieve("Sequelae of Fetal Alcohol Syndrome")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the gathered evidence, I concur with the initial reasoning presented earlier. Macrocephaly is indeed unusual given the nature of Fetal Alcohol Syndrome (FAS); the main characteristics involve microcephaly (reduced head circumference due to poor brain development), thinned corpus callosum (a structure connecting cerebral hemispheres affected by FAS), and holoprosencephaly (underdeveloped forebrain resulting in facial deformities).
Furthermore, the review confirms that FAS primarily manifests craniofacially as smaller-than-normal heads rather than enlarged ones. Therefore, considering all the evidence on craniofacial anatomy alterations in relation to FAS, \boxed{A} remains correct since none of the structural variations like macrocephaly fit amongst common observed traits of FAS compared to other listed conditions.
This reaffirms Agent1 and Agent2's conclusions about distinguishing features between macrocephaly and typical sequelae of FAS based on consistent medical literature and observations over decades studying these syndromes thoroughly. No new compelling evidence emerged challenging existing consensus pointing toward macrocephaly exclusion distinctly non-conforming behaviorally against standard profiles characteristically displayed across large sample sizes referenced today concerning FAS manifestation sets globally.
